TEXT.BibTeX_citation: @misc{Wiki_szy, title = {Sakizaya Wikipedia}, author = {{Wikipedia contributors}}, year = {2026}, publisher = {Wikimedia Foundation}, url = {https://szy.wikipedia.org/}, urldate = {2026-06-09} }

S.FORM: == balatbat == ťĖÉšļģ balatbat ku kalapiyat i kelekelengan. siwkay nu balatbat balatbat sa, u lamal edil ideng saca cilu', sawsawni malikat sawsawni mapaneng. azih nu mita, balatbat sa. u bunac i tapuku i labi, mabaltbat tuway. u sulit nu Amilika A star is a luminous sphere of plasma held together by its own gravity. The nearest star to Earth is the Sun. Many other stars are visible to the naked eye from Earth during the night, appearing as a multitude of fixed luminous points in the sky due to their immense distance from Earth. Historically, the most prominent stars were grouped into constellations and asterisms, the brightest of which gained proper names. Astronomers have assembled star catalogues that identify the known stars and provide standardized stellar designations. However, most of the stars in the Universe, including all stars outside our galaxy, the Milky Way, are invisible to the naked eye from Earth. Indeed, most are invisible from Earth even through the most powerful telescopes.
S.PHON: == bar~…ĺatbat == ** bar~…ĺatbat ku kar~…ĺapijat i k…ôr~…ĺ…ôk…ôr~…ĺ…ôŇčan. siwkaj nu bar~…ĺatbat bar~…ĺatbat sa, u r~…ĺamar~…ĺ …ôdir~…ĺ id…ôŇč sa ¶a ¶ir~…ĺu Ē~ °, sawsawni mar~…ĺikat sawsawni mapan…ôŇč. azińß nu mita, bar~…ĺatbat sa. u buna ¶ i tapuku i r~…ĺabi, mabar~…ĺtbat tuwaj. u sur~…ĺit nu amir~…ĺika a star is a r~…ĺuminous spńß…ôr…ô of pr~…ĺasma ńß…ôr~…ĺd tog…ôtńß…ôr bj its own gra*itj. tńß…ô n…ôar…ôst star to …ôartńß is tńß…ô sun. manj otńß…ôr stars ar…ô *isibr~…ĺ…ô to tńß…ô nak…ôd …ô…ô from …ôartńß duriŇč tńß…ô nigńßt, app…ôariŇč as a mur~…ĺtitud…ô of fi*…ôd r~…ĺuminous points in tńß…ô skj du…ô to tńß…ôir imm…ôns…ô distan ¶…ô from …ôartńß. ńßistori ¶ar~…ĺr~…ĺj, tńß…ô most promin…ônt stars w…ôr…ô group…ôd into ¶onst…ôr~…ĺr~…ĺations and ast…ôrisms, tńß…ô brigńßt…ôst of wńßi ¶ńß gain…ôd prop…ôr nam…ôs. astronom…ôrs ńßa*…ô ass…ômbr~…ĺ…ôd star ¶atar~…ĺogu…ôs tńßat id…ôntifj tńß…ô known stars and pro*id…ô standardiz…ôd st…ôr~…ĺr~…ĺar d…ôsignations. ńßow…ô*…ôr, most of tńß…ô stars in tńß…ô uni*…ôrs…ô, in ¶r~…ĺudiŇč ar~…ĺr~…ĺ stars outsid…ô our gar~…ĺa*j, tńß…ô mir~…ĺkj waj, ar…ô in*isibr~…ĺ…ô to tńß…ô nak…ôd …ô…ô from …ôartńß. ind…ô…ôd, most ar…ô in*isibr~…ĺ…ô from …ôartńß …ô*…ôn tńßrougńß tńß…ô most pow…ôrfur~…ĺ t…ôr~…ĺ…ôs ¶op…ôs.
S.FORM: == balatbat == ťĖÉšļģ balatbat ku kalapiyat i kelekelengan. siwkay nu balatbat balatbat sa, u lamal edil ideng saca cilu', sawsawni malikat sawsawni mapaneng. azih nu mita, balatbat sa. u bunac i tapuku i labi, mabaltbat tuway. u sulit nu Amilika A star is a luminous sphere of plasma held together by its own gravity. The nearest star to Earth is the Sun. Many other stars are visible to the naked eye from Earth during the night, appearing as a multitude of fixed luminous points in the sky due to their immense distance from Earth. Historically, the most prominent stars were grouped into constellations and asterisms, the brightest of which gained proper names. Astronomers have assembled star catalogues that identify the known stars and provide standardized stellar designations. However, most of the stars in the Universe, including all stars outside our galaxy, the Milky Way, are invisible to the naked eye from Earth. Indeed, most are invisible from Earth even through the most powerful telescopes.
S.PHON: == bar~…ĺatbat == ** bar~…ĺatbat ku kar~…ĺapijat i k…ôr~…ĺ…ôk…ôr~…ĺ…ôŇčan. siwkaj nu bar~…ĺatbat bar~…ĺatbat sa, u r~…ĺamar~…ĺ …ôdir~…ĺ id…ôŇč sa ¶a ¶ir~…ĺu Ē~ °, sawsawni mar~…ĺikat sawsawni mapan…ôŇč. azińß nu mita, bar~…ĺatbat sa. u buna ¶ i tapuku i r~…ĺabi, mabar~…ĺtbat tuwaj. u sur~…ĺit nu amir~…ĺika a star is a r~…ĺuminous spńß…ôr…ô of pr~…ĺasma ńß…ôr~…ĺd tog…ôtńß…ôr bj its own gra*itj. tńß…ô n…ôar…ôst star to …ôartńß is tńß…ô sun. manj otńß…ôr stars ar…ô *isibr~…ĺ…ô to tńß…ô nak…ôd …ô…ô from …ôartńß duriŇč tńß…ô nigńßt, app…ôariŇč as a mur~…ĺtitud…ô of fi*…ôd r~…ĺuminous points in tńß…ô skj du…ô to tńß…ôir imm…ôns…ô distan ¶…ô from …ôartńß. ńßistori ¶ar~…ĺr~…ĺj, tńß…ô most promin…ônt stars w…ôr…ô group…ôd into ¶onst…ôr~…ĺr~…ĺations and ast…ôrisms, tńß…ô brigńßt…ôst of wńßi ¶ńß gain…ôd prop…ôr nam…ôs. astronom…ôrs ńßa*…ô ass…ômbr~…ĺ…ôd star ¶atar~…ĺogu…ôs tńßat id…ôntifj tńß…ô known stars and pro*id…ô standardiz…ôd st…ôr~…ĺr~…ĺar d…ôsignations. ńßow…ô*…ôr, most of tńß…ô stars in tńß…ô uni*…ôrs…ô, in ¶r~…ĺudiŇč ar~…ĺr~…ĺ stars outsid…ô our gar~…ĺa*j, tńß…ô mir~…ĺkj waj, ar…ô in*isibr~…ĺ…ô to tńß…ô nak…ôd …ô…ô from …ôartńß. ind…ô…ôd, most ar…ô in*isibr~…ĺ…ô from …ôartńß …ô*…ôn tńßrougńß tńß…ô most pow…ôrfur~…ĺ t…ôr~…ĺ…ôs ¶op…ôs.
